summary: Shapiro shares a practical woodworking tip about filling holes in walnut burl using clear epoxy. He walks through the iterative problem-solving process: epoxy drains through holes that penetrate the board, taping the back traps air bubbles, and the solution is a pinhole in the tape that gets sealed once epoxy starts dripping. It's a short, hands-on maker post from early in his blogging history.
key_points: Clear epoxy can fill burl holes but drains through if the hole penetrates the board
Taping the back traps air and creates bubbles; a pinhole solves both drainage and air problems
Epoxy fumes are deceptively dangerous because they're nearly odorless but toxic
money_quotes: It turns out that if you have a hole that goes all the way through the board, epoxy (even the thick stuff) will drain right through to the other side.
It turns out that if you do that, there's nowhere for the air trapped in the hole to go, and you wind up with a big bubble at the bottom of the hole.
Cover the bottom with tape, but make a pinhole. When epoxy starts to drip out, cover the pinhole with a second piece of tape.
key_insight: Iterative problem-solving in hands-on making often means discovering that each fix introduces a new constraint, and the best solutions address multiple problems elegantly.
